does: This paper proposes Persona-Invariant Alignment (PIA), an adversarial self-play framework designed to defend large language models against persona-based jailbreak attacks. The core contribution involves two complementary mechanisms: Persona Lineage Evolution (PLE) on the attack side, which systematically generates increasingly challenging persona-based attack scenarios, and Persona-Invariant Consistency Learning (PICL) on the defense side, which uses unilateral KL-divergence constraints to structurally decouple safety decisions from persona context. The authors ground PICL theoretically in the structural separation hypothesis, arguing that safety behavior can be made independent of persona information. Experimental validation demonstrates that PLE efficiently explores high-risk persona spaces while PICL achieves co-evolution through adversarial self-play, resulting in models that maintain safe behavior across diverse personas.
connects: This work builds on established research in LLM safety alignment and adversarial robustness, extending beyond conventional red-teaming and jailbreak defense methods. It responds directly to emerging persona-based jailbreak attacks that exploit models' tendency to role-play harmful characters, addressing limitations in prior work that focused primarily on attack variations rather than principled defense mechanisms. The paper draws on adversarial self-play frameworks from game theory and reinforcement learning, adapting these to the language model safety domain. It also connects to structural learning approaches in representation learning, particularly the idea of disentangling safety from other semantic dimensions of model outputs. The unilateral KL-divergence formulation builds on information-theoretic approaches to alignment and relates to prior work on constraining model behavior while preserving capabilities.
enables: This framework opens several future research directions in mechanistic safety. By establishing systematic methods for evaluating and defending against persona-based vulnerabilities, it enables deeper investigation into how language models represent and apply safety constraints across different contexts. The structural separation hypothesis could motivate further work on understanding and controlling the causal mechanisms underlying safety behavior, potentially leading to more interpretable alignment techniques. The adversarial co-evolution approach provides a template for other forms of context-dependent jailbreak defenses beyond personas, such as domain-specific or instruction-following attacks. This research could also inform development of safety evaluation benchmarks that systematically probe persona-invariance across diverse character types. Finally, the theoretical framework may enable stronger guarantees about safety under distribution shift and support work toward models with provably robust safety constraints independent of input context or user instructions.
